title: Civil Engineering Visual Library
subtitle: The Rodgers Playbook · Internal Training Series
tagline: Creating communities with clients who share our values.

modules.title: Planning / Pre-Development
modules.description: How a parcel becomes a development.

slides.title: Raw Land vs. Concept Plan Overlay
slides.subtitle: Seeing the vision before anything is built

images.label: Raw land — pre-development aerial
Concept plan overlay — proposed use

slides.keyLesson: A concept plan turns a blank site into a development story. Every lot, road, and basin begins as an idea on top of a real piece of land.

teaching.t1: Left: an aerial of raw, undeveloped land. Right: the same parcel with a concept plan overlay showing lots, roads, and open space.
teaching.t2: The concept plan is the first translation of ownership boundaries and site constraints into a buildable layout.
teaching.t3: Every line you draft later — utilities, grading, storm — exists because someone first decided what the site becomes.
teaching.t4: Notice how the concept respects drainage, access points, and easements. Nothing is drawn at random.
teaching.t5: Design starts with land. Always know the parcel before you touch the plan.

slides.title: Existing Topo vs. Proposed Development Plan
slides.subtitle: Topography is destiny

images.label: Existing topographic survey
Proposed development plan

slides.keyLesson: Topography dictates the design. Proposed grading, drainage, and road alignments all trace back to the existing contours.

modules.title: Surveying & Existing Conditions
modules.description: The foundation of every CAD drawing.

slides.title: Survey Crew in the Field vs. Survey Points in CAD
slides.subtitle: Where every CAD drawing actually starts

images.label: Survey crew using total station on site
Survey point cloud / linework in CAD

slides.keyLesson: Every point in CAD started as a person standing in the field. The office drawing is only as good as the field data behind it.

teaching.t1: Left: a two-person crew running a total station. Right: the resulting point file and linework opened in CAD.
teaching.t2: Field data becomes CAD data. Point numbers, descriptions, and codes carry directly from the instrument to the drawing.
teaching.t3: If your CAD base is wrong, every downstream design is wrong. Surveyors are the foundation of accuracy.
teaching.t4: Notice the descriptors on points — BOC, TOC, EP, IP. These codes tell you what each measurement represents.
teaching.t5: Never disrespect the survey. That is the ground truth of the project.

slides.title: Control Point Photo vs. Survey File View
slides.subtitle: The hidden anchor of every project

images.label: Rebar-and-cap control monument
Same control point in the CAD file

slides.keyLesson: Control points tie the site to a coordinate system. Lose the control, lose the project.

modules.title: Site Layout / Grading
modules.description: Reshaping the dirt to make a site work.

slides.title: Existing Contours vs. Proposed Grading Plan
slides.subtitle: Reshaping the dirt

images.label: Existing contours from the survey
Proposed grading plan with finished contours

slides.keyLesson: Grading is the art of reshaping dirt. Proposed contours tell the contractor exactly where to cut and where to fill.

slides.title: Earthwork in the Field vs. Grading Plan
slides.subtitle: The plan becomes piles of dirt

images.label: Dozer and scraper moving dirt
Same area on the grading plan

slides.keyLesson: That grading plan becomes real piles of dirt. Volumes matter — cut and fill imbalance costs money.

slides.title: Road Plan View vs. Profile View
slides.subtitle: Every road exists in two dimensions of design

images.label: Road plan — horizontal alignment
Road profile — vertical alignment

slides.keyLesson: A road exists in two dimensions of design: plan (left-right) and profile (up-down). You must design them together.

teaching.t1: Left: the horizontal alignment in plan view, showing curves and stationing. Right: the vertical profile showing grade changes.
teaching.t2: Plan controls where the road goes. Profile controls how the road rises and falls.
teaching.t3: K-values, stopping sight distance, and drainage all depend on the profile.
teaching.t4: Notice the stationing lines up on both views.
teaching.t5: Never design a road in plan alone. Always check profile.

slides.title: Cross-Section vs. Built Roadway
slides.subtitle: A cross-section is a recipe

images.label: Typical roadway cross-section
Finished road showing each layer in real life

slides.keyLesson: A cross-section is a recipe. Every line becomes a lift of asphalt, a sub-base, or a curb poured in the field.

slides.title: Corridor Model vs. Actual Road Construction
slides.subtitle: The digital road drives the real one

images.label: 3D corridor model in Civil 3D
Grading crew building to the model

slides.keyLesson: 3D models are now built directly by machine control. The digital road drives the real one.

modules.title: Stormwater / Drainage
modules.description: Pipes, inlets, and ponds — from symbol to structure.

slides.title: Catch Basin in the Field vs. Storm Structure on Plan
slides.subtitle: Connecting the symbol to the structure

images.label: Installed catch basin at curb
Storm structure symbol on the plan

slides.keyLesson: Every symbol on a storm plan is a real structure someone has to cast, set, and connect. Symbols are shorthand for reality.

teaching.t1: Left: a precast catch basin installed at a curb inlet. Right: the same structure called out on the plan with a structure ID and rim/invert.
teaching.t2: The symbol on the left is what you see every day driving. The symbol on the right is how we communicate it to the contractor.
teaching.t3: If the rim or invert on the plan is wrong, the structure is set wrong. Water does not forgive mistakes.
teaching.t4: Notice the structure ID, rim elevation, and pipe inverts. That tiny callout is the entire build spec.
teaching.t5: When you draft a structure, picture the real thing. Every number matters.

slides.title: Pipe Trench vs. Pipe Network in CAD
slides.subtitle: Storm flows by gravity

images.label: Open trench with bedding and RCP installed
Pipe network in CAD with slopes and inverts

slides.keyLesson: Storm pipes flow by gravity. Slope is not optional — the plan must match what gets built.

slides.title: Detention Pond vs. Stormwater Plan
slides.subtitle: Calculated volumes, not eyeballed holes

images.label: Constructed detention pond with outfall structure
Stormwater plan showing pond contours and routing

slides.keyLesson: Ponds are calculated volumes, not eyeballed holes. Every elevation contour carries acre-feet of storage.

modules.title: Water & Sewer Utilities
modules.description: Hydrants, valves, sewer profiles, and the parts that make a community.

slides.title: Fire Hydrant vs. Utility Plan
slides.subtitle: Symbol placement saves lives

images.label: Fire hydrant in place on curb
Hydrant symbol on the utility plan

slides.keyLesson: Hydrant spacing protects lives. The symbol placement on the plan directly determines where real hydrants go.

slides.title: Sewer Trench vs. Utility Profile
slides.subtitle: Gravity is unforgiving

images.label: Deep sewer trench with pipe being laid
Sanitary sewer profile showing inverts and slopes

slides.keyLesson: Sanitary sewer works on gravity and minimum slopes. The profile is the build instruction — get it right or dig twice.

slides.title: Valve / Fitting vs. Plan Symbol
slides.subtitle: Real parts, real orders

images.label: Water valve box and fittings on site
Same valve shown as a symbol on the utility plan

slides.keyLesson: Water systems are assemblies of standard fittings. Every tee, cross, and valve on plan is a real part someone orders.

modules.title: Site Features / Details
modules.description: ADA, sidewalks, striping — the human-scale details.

slides.title: ADA Ramp Photo vs. ADA Detail Drawing
slides.subtitle: A quarter-inch matters

images.label: Built ADA ramp with truncated domes
ADA ramp detail showing slopes and dimensions

slides.keyLesson: ADA details are legal requirements. A quarter-inch out of tolerance can trigger a rebuild.

slides.title: Sidewalk Photo vs. Site Plan
slides.subtitle: Connecting the site to the neighborhood

images.label: Finished sidewalk with joints and curb
Sidewalk layout on the site plan

slides.keyLesson: Sidewalks connect the site to its neighbors. The plan defines width, joints, and slope — not just a line.

slides.title: Parking Striping vs. Pavement Detail
slides.subtitle: Paint follows geometry

images.label: Fresh parking lot striping
Striping and pavement detail

slides.keyLesson: Paint on asphalt looks simple but follows strict geometry. Details control stall size, ADA stalls, and traffic flow.

slides.title: Silt Fence in the Field vs. ESC Plan
slides.subtitle: Keeping mud out of streams

images.label: Installed silt fence along perimeter
Silt fence shown on ESC plan

slides.keyLesson: ESC is how we keep mud out of streams. The plan is a legal document that inspectors check against the field.

slides.title: Inlet Protection Photo vs. ESC Detail
slides.subtitle: Right install, right detail

images.label: Inlet protection installed at a catch basin
ESC detail for the same device

slides.keyLesson: Each ESC device has a right and wrong install. The detail is the playbook the contractor works from.

modules.title: Construction Phase
modules.description: Documents become the contractor's marching orders.

slides.title: Excavation Photo vs. Construction Plan
slides.subtitle: Documents become marching orders

images.label: Mass excavation on site
Grading / construction plan for same area

slides.keyLesson: Construction documents become the contractor's marching orders. Clarity in drafting equals speed in the field.

slides.title: Pipe Install vs. Utility Drawing
slides.subtitle: The field trusts the plan

images.label: Crew installing storm or sewer pipe
Utility plan with pipe callouts

slides.keyLesson: The field trusts the plan. When they hit conflicts, they stop. Good drafting prevents that stop.

slides.title: Paving Operation vs. Roadway Detail
slides.subtitle: Lifts and depths are sacred

images.label: Paver laying asphalt
Pavement section detail

slides.keyLesson: Pavement lifts, depths, and compaction all come from the detail. Shortcutting any of it shortens the pavement life.

slides.title: Finished Neighborhood vs. Original Concept
slides.subtitle: The full lifecycle, in one view

images.label: Finished neighborhood — aerial
Original concept layout

slides.keyLesson: Every finished neighborhood traces back to a concept plan. The closer the concept matches reality, the better the team did.

slides.title: Finished Roadway vs. Design Drawing
slides.subtitle: Great design disappears

images.label: Completed, paved and striped roadway
Road design drawing

slides.keyLesson: Great design disappears into the landscape. If it works, people never notice it.

slides.title: Final Site vs. Grading / Drainage Concept
slides.subtitle: A site that drains is a site that lasts

images.label: Final site — dry, functioning, landscaped
Grading and drainage concept that made it work

slides.keyLesson: A site that drains properly is a site that lasts. Everything you learn here shows up in someone's backyard.
